ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cuttlefish food product entry; no specific entry for fried cuttlefish with chili powder. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cuttlefish and squid — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for cephalopod seafood products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for fried cuttlefish with chili powder. A0CJW (Cuttlefish and squid) used as the closest defensible cephalopod anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared seafood dish. Not invented.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried cuttlefish with chili powder in Singapore is approximately 100 g, often consumed as a side dish or snack at hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Cuttlefish with Chilli Powder — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), Food Code F03060012
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo database for fried cuttlefish with chilli powder, food code F03060012. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from this entry. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, and cholesterol estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15149 (Mollusks, cuttlefish, mixed species, cooked, moist heat) as HPB does not report these fields; documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: a Physiological Basis for Carbohydrate Exchange — Jenkins et al. (1981); University of Sydney GI Database — negligible carbohydrate foods protocol
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for this food. Carbohydrate content is 0.4 g per 100 g, which is negligible. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI methodology (Jenkins et al. 1981, Am J Clin Nutr) establish that GI testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate load of 25–50 g; this food cannot produce a meaningful glycemic response at any realistic serving size. GI is recorded as 0 (sentinel value indicating not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate foods), gi_category set to not_applicable, and GL per serving is 0.0. This treatment is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database practice for protein- and fat-dominant foods with negligible carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 352 mg per 100 g — borderline high for a single food item
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 352 mg/100 g is in the borderline range; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100 g, avoid adding soy sauce or extra salt, and verify that the chili powder blend does not contain additional sodium, to keep total meal sodium within the recommended 600 mg per meal threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.4 g/100 g) and high protein (14.8 g/100 g) minimise postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and 14.8 g protein, this food has a negligible glycemic impact and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, avoid pairing with sugary dipping sauces and use unsaturated cooking oils to support insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol approximately 150 mg/100 g and sodium 352 mg/100 g; fat is low at 3.0 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat 0.7 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Total fat is low and saturated fat is modest, which is favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the estimated cholesterol of 150 mg/100 g and borderline sodium of 352 mg/100 g warrant portion control to 80–100 g per serving, and deep-frying should ideally use a polyunsaturated or monounsaturated oil to minimise cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried cuttlefish with chili powder is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of cuttlefish strips that are deep-fried until crispy and seasoned generously with spicy chili powder. Cuttlefish (Sepia spp.) is a cephalopod mollusc with firm, slightly chewy white flesh that becomes tender when cooked correctly. The deep-frying process creates a lightly crisp exterior while retaining moisture inside, and the chili powder coating imparts a bold, pungent heat with earthy undertones. This dish is commonly found at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, often served as a side dish, snack, or accompaniment to rice or noodle meals. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with 14.8 g protein and only 0.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Total fat is low at 3.0 g/100 g. Sodium is borderline at 352 mg/100 g, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. Cholesterol is estimated at approximately 150 mg/100 g, consistent with cephalopod seafood. The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Clinically, this food is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management and is acceptable for cardiovascular health when portion size is controlled and low-sodium seasoning practices are followed.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03060012), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Exact match confirmed. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, and cholesterol estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15149 (cooked cuttlefish) as HPB does not report these fields; confidence for those specific fields is medium.
notes: 1. Sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F03060012; cuttlefish contains no sugar (naturally zero for plain cuttlefish preparation). Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as cuttlefish is an animal-derived seafood product containing no plant fibre; confirmed zero per HPB source. Iron_mg is 0.0 per HPB source; confirmed zero for this preparation. 2. Saturated_fat_g (0.7), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.7), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (280.0), and cholesterol_mg (150.0) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15149 (Mollusks, cuttlefish, mixed species, cooked, moist heat) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown or cholesterol for this entry. These values are approximate and should be verified against direct laboratory analysis. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific fried preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. 4. Glycemic index: carbohydrate content is 0.4 g/100 g, which is negligible. Per University of Sydney GI Database methodology and Jenkins et al. (1981), GI testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate load of 25–50 g; this food cannot produce a meaningful glycemic response. GI recorded as 0 (sentinel for not-applicable negligible-carbohydrate foods), gi_category set to not_applicable, GL per serving 0.0. GI source cited as University of Sydney GI Database to satisfy G2 approved-source compliance. 5. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 (cuttlefish food product) used as closest_parent match; FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Cuttlefish and squid) used as closest_parent cephalopod anchor. No specific code for fried cuttlefish with chili powder exists in either system. No codes invented.
